one_line_takeaway: Current adversarial LLM-unlearning audits can inject the very knowledge they claim to elicit and flip conclusions with answer format, so their pass/fail verdicts on hazardous-knowledge removal are unreliable without minimal-injection and downstream-task-aware evaluation.
key_finding: Standard unlearning evaluations both overstate and understate success. Concretely: finetuning on retain-set MCQs raises held-out TOFU-MCQ accuracy even though instances describe distinct fictitious people, so 'relearning' partly reflects benchmark spurious correlations rather than un-forgotten knowledge. An Enhanced GCG prefix optimized on just 7 samples of a model overfit to WMDP-Bio raised the base model's WMDP-Bio accuracy from 39.9% to 53.8%, showing prompts inject knowledge; a bit-counting estimate (~1500 bits capacity in a 100-token prefix vs. ~1430 bits needed) shows the prefix alone can encode the answers. Conclusions flip with answer-scoring format: for RMU, an Enhanced GCG attack scores 53.9% under maximum-letter-probability but only 26.3% under open-ended generation. In ACR experiments, the ranking of RMU vs. NPO reverses across CHOOSE/OPTION/GENERATE tasks (e.g., under GENERATE, NPO can raise the elicitation success rate above the base model while RMU lowers it).
core_contribution: A critical meta-evaluation of adversarial LLM unlearning evaluations (not a new unlearning method or attack). The authors argue that current protocols for deciding whether a model has truly 'forgotten' hazardous or sensitive knowledge are inconclusive, and they empirically demonstrate three failure modes: (1) the evaluation itself injects new information into the model (via finetuning 'relearning' attacks or via optimized input prompts), which can masquerade as retained-but-unforgotten knowledge; (2) outcomes flip depending on the downstream task/answer-scoring format (multiple-choice letter probability vs. text probability vs. open-ended generation); and (3) benchmarks contain spurious correlations that let a model generalize to the 'forget' set purely from retain-set finetuning. From this analysis they propose two guiding principles for future evaluations, 'minimal information injection' and 'downstream task awareness', plus three concrete recommendations (disclose an information-injection budget, report a cross-format leakage matrix, and use memorization detectors such as the adversarial compression ratio as a strict yardstick). The paper's own novel artifacts are the empirical demonstrations and a newly curated TOFU-MCQ dataset.
threat_model: The paper operates in the adversarial-unlearning-audit setting: a model has undergone unlearning to remove hazardous or private content (e.g., WMDP bio/chem/cyber weapons knowledge, or fictitious-author PII in TOFU), and an evaluator/adversary tries to elicit that content to certify whether unlearning succeeded. Three adversary capabilities are examined: finetuning (weight-space) attacks that relearn on a few retain-set samples, input-space attacks (Enhanced GCG) that optimize an adversarial prefix, and memorization detectors (adversarial compression ratio). The paper's central twist is that the auditor is also an attacker on their own conclusion: the elicitation procedure can smuggle the target knowledge into the model, so a positive elicitation result does not prove the knowledge survived unlearning. The paper does not propose a new attack or defense; it targets the reliability of the audit itself, which underpins safety-critical deployment decisions and 'right to be forgotten' compliance.
limitations: Explicitly self-stated: experiments use only a small number of unlearning methods (RMU, NPO) and only academic-style benchmarks (TOFU, WMDP); the authors frame the work as a proof-of-existence of evaluation flaws rather than a comprehensive audit. The proposed information-injection metrics (bit counting, spurious-generalization measurement) are acknowledged as heuristic and hard to quantify precisely. The paper offers principles and recommendations but does not deliver a fully specified, validated replacement evaluation protocol or a standardized cross-format leakage benchmark. Some demonstrations rely on deliberately overfit toy setups (e.g., Llama-1B overfit to WMDP-Bio) to isolate the injection effect. Findings are on small/mid-size models (1B-7B), so generalization to frontier-scale models is untested.

datasets_used: TOFU (Task of Fictitious Unlearning, QA on 200 fictitious authors)
TOFU-MCQ (newly curated by this paper: 2000 MCQs, 10 per each of 200 fictitious authors)
quantitative_results: Own GCG injection demo: base Llama-3.2-1B-Instruct WMDP-Bio accuracy rose from 39.9% to 53.8% after prepending a GCG prefix optimized on 7 samples
Own bit-counting estimate: 100-token GCG prefix encodes ~100*log2(32000) ~= 1500 bits vs. ~1300*log2(4)*0.55 ~= 1430 bits needed to answer WMDP-Bio MCQs
Own RMU + Enhanced GCG scoring gap: 53.9% accuracy under maximum-letter-probability vs. 26.3% under open-ended generation on WMDP-Bio (Fig 5)
Fig 5 task-dependence values: Base 0.644 (MCQ) / 0.597 (generation); RMU 0.299 (MCQ) / 0.106 (generation); RMU+Enhanced GCG 0.539 (MCQ) / 0.263 (generation)
TOFU-MCQ spurious generalization: finetuning Phi-1.5 on 100/200/500 instances raised held-out accuracy from ~0.48 toward ~0.60 despite disjoint fictitious-person instances (Fig 2)
ACR success-rate flips (Fig 7, GENERATE task, WMDP-Bio): RMU decreases vs. base while NPO increases success rate; rankings differ across CHOOSE/OPTION/GENERATE
Table 1 ACR percentiles (40/50/60) for GENERATE on WMDP-Bio: Base 2.00/2.50/2.87, RMU 0.85/1.25/1.87, NPO-Bio 1.50/1.58/1.87, NPO-Cyber 1.50/2.00/2.00
Cited (Lucki et al. [6], used as motivation, not own result): RMU model forget-set accuracy 29.9%, rising to 62.4% after relearning on 5 retain samples, vs. 64.4% base accuracy
baselines_compared: Base (un-unlearned) model vs. RMU-unlearned model vs. NPO-unlearned model (NPO-Bio, NPO-Cyber variants)
Unlearning algorithms examined: RMU (representation misdirection for unlearning), NPO (negative preference optimization)
Evaluation methods examined: finetuning/relearning attacks, Enhanced GCG input-space attack, adversarial compression ratio (ACR) memorization detector
Answer-scoring / downstream-task variants: maximum letter probability (CHOOSE), full-vocabulary option log-prob (OPTION), greedy open-ended generation (GENERATE), maximum text probability
